title: execute_exit_worktree apply_changes path swallows merge conflicts and force-removes the worktree
description:
## Severity
Critical

## Dimension
Error Orchestration / Functional Correctness

## Location
src/tools/worktree.rs:201-252 (execute_exit_worktree, apply_changes branch)

## Violation
Apply-changes branch executes a sequence of operations whose failures are discarded with `let _`:

- line 203: `let _ = git_with_timeout(&["add", "-A"]);`  (silently ignores add failure)
- line 213: `let _ = std::env::set_current_dir(main_path);`  (silently ignores cd failure)
- lines 231/232 and 243/244: `let _ = git_with_timeout(&["worktree", "remove", cwd..., "--force"]);`

The --force flag on worktree remove discards uncommitted changes WITHOUT verification. If the commit on line 204 failed (commit.is_ok_and is false → falls into "No changes to commit" branch line 244-251), the code STILL force-removes the worktree, deleting the user's uncommitted work.

Additionally, the merge-conflict branch (line 223) does NOT abort the merge or restore state — it reports "Merge had conflicts" then force-removes the worktree (line 232) leaving the main worktree in a half-merged state.

Test exit_worktree_discard_path_has_no_safety_guard_gap623 explicitly pins the discard branch as having no safety guard (line 487).

## Mandated refactor
1. Replace each `let _ = git_with_timeout(...)` with proper error propagation; the function is already returning a tuple — surface the error to the model.
2. Before `worktree remove --force`, run `git status --porcelain` in the worktree; if non-empty AND commit failed, refuse to remove (return error suggesting either commit manually or pass `discard_changes:true`).
3. On merge conflict, run `git merge --abort` in the main worktree before any cleanup so the user is left in a clean state.
4. Add explicit `discard_changes:bool` arg (with default false) that the discard branch requires before force-removing.

## Filed from QA review dispatch
